reviews.review_text:
Great little gem.  However a big issue is the lack of any sort of multiplayer.  LAN and Direct IP official multiplayer would be amazing.  

Please add Direct IP/LAN multiplayer developers.

From Factory Hater to Cosmic Conqueror (1,000+ Hours Later)

Recommended
Total Playtime: 1,089.9 hrs (128/128 Achievements)
The Hook: From Intimidated to Addicted
I’ll admit it: I initially disliked this game. As someone who didn’t naturally possess a "factory-loving" gaming mind, the sheer complexity felt utterly overwhelming at first. I only pushed through because I wanted to explore the gorgeous planets and finally escape Midterra. But once the logic of the logistics networks clicked? I was completely hooked, and I still can’t get enough. The visuals are consistently astonishing, the customization of the factories is incredibly satisfying, and the underlying logic of the game’s automation is absolutely pristine.

The 30x Difficulty "Truce" Strategy & Metadata Economy
To give you an idea of how far this game can scale, I am currently running a massive endgame setup pumping out 28.8k White Cubes per minute on a brutal 30x Difficulty.

If you want a massive tip for tackling 30x difficulty, here is a pro-strat "cheat": Buy a truce with the Dark Fog early on. Use that peaceful breathing room to aggressively build up your factory and your defensive grid. Once your guns are completely set up, betray the truce and wipe them out. Investing around 10k of white metadata to secure a massive 30x return on your investment later on is an absolute no-brainer.

The combat makes sense contextually, but it isn't my favorite part of the loop. When I was sitting at under 100k metadata, the extra boost was nice. Now, floating near 1 million metadata, I don’t see much point to it outside of the exclusive enemy drops. And even with that much metadata currency in the bank, I still can’t bring myself to buy the Dark Fog loot that the planetary bases just drop for free anyway.

The Endgame Flex: Breaking the Simulation
My current setup features an entire planet completely covered in Ray Receivers pumping photons continuously. The optimization in this game is generally incredible—I can maintain a flawless 4K at 120 FPS by hiding my spheres and halting solar sail launches. But if I dare unhide my fully realized Dyson Spheres? It instantly drops my Ryzen 9950X3D and RTX 5090 down to 12 FPS. That is the ultimate performance benchmark.

My Biggest Grievance & A Balancing Suggestion
What I disliked the most about the late-game loop is the sheer, tedious grind of the eradication phase if you want to permanently clear a system. It mandatorily takes over 2 hours of active micromanagement to completely clean a single system with 3 orbital hives.

I strongly feel the developers should introduce an endgame superweapon to automate this devastation. To keep it perfectly balanced, it shouldn't be easy: make it require Core Elements (the ultimate Dark Fog drop) to build and consume, alongside a massive, continuous energy consumption penalty. This ensures no player can just wipe hives out without knowing the grueling struggle of doing it by hand the first time.

Even if this weapon takes the same 2 hours—or hell, double the time (4 hours) to charge up—it would be infinitely better if I could just let it charge while I focus on tuning other parts of my supply chain. I wouldn’t even mind being trapped in the same system while it charges; I just want to automate the annihilation instead of babysitting the cleanup.

Final Verdict: 10/10
Despite my gripes with the tedious nature of late-game Hive clearing, Dyson Sphere Program is an absolute masterpiece. It took someone who hated automation games and turned them into a galactic engineer obsessed with universal optimization. If you are on the fence because it looks too complex, push through. The payoff is astronomical.

Beauty and intuition in a game meets a scientific theme that is so wonderfully explored that - game aside, marks this a unique life experience worth having. Very fairly priced for such a detailed and nurtured game.

If you are familiar with the factory genre, this game is one to witness, if not for its detail, it's depth, and it's accessible complexity, then for the love the developers poured into making this a great player experience in a compelling world. 

This game is more suitable for intermediate-advanced computer users - people who have difficulty using computers in general, but especially menu navigation might find that this game's many menus present a barrier to being able to experience the full depth this game has to offer. Doesn't mean you won't enjoy it, and hey, it might improve your efficacy at using computers in general, but I'd feel bad if I let my friend buy it for their young child only for their child to be unable to engage with the media in a meaningful way, for example. This disclaimer probably doesn't apply to you if you're reading this review, but might save someone from making a purchase that doesn't suit them. If this disclaimer didn't apply to you and you're still reading this - you should get the game. Great devs. Great game. get the game. as a little treat. you deserve a little treat. this game could be that treat. especially if you like space, or science, or factory games etc. goodbye. Live life the best you can.

I love the power system, the way the game handles oil, with possibility of zero hydrogen or oil byproduct (though energetic graphite could pose an issue). The solar swarm system seemed horrible at first but when I set up 450 solar sails p/m I managed to produce 10-50x the original power of my main planet for every single planet. It's also really easy to use solar swarms to set up forge worlds in different star systems. Infinite resources may be frowned upon but I love setting up a factory and not worrying about ores
Eexcellent game. Very addicted and can be a huge timesink in the best possible way. Love it,

If you like Automation games and Space.... most definitely the game for you
If you are obsessive over details or symmetry like myself, its a nightmare buuuuut.... soo much fun

its been a year with no content or development, devolpers post news about holidays but nothing about the game, been over a year and 1/2 since they mentioned more content and its been silence since. the game is fun and theres lots to be played but untill there are real updates the game feels abandond 80%  complete

I was pessimistic at first. But then played it for hours. Then I know why it got 97% score from 82k+ users.

This is a phenomenal game. It combines the concept of factory games with interstellar management.

It has tons of products which you can produce and lots of facilities you can build. The complexity rises as time goes by and as you expand your factory empire. Every new research unlocks useful things.

The pace of the game is perfect and fully customized based on your own pace. You can choose to create products from factories or craft them with your own built-in replicator. You have various ways of handling product distribution, either land-based or air-based.

The graphic quality is good. You can basically put thousands of factories across tens or hundreds of planets. With high-spec PC, it's just fantastic to see your Industrial Empire set in motion across multiple star systems.

It's just a phenomenal game. It should be the best factory game for this entire decade.

Doesnt look like it will cross the finishline. Started out great and its a semi novel idea. But the further time has gone the seemingly less coherent the development has become. While the bones and meat are there the meals not ready yet and the chef isnt looking so hot. Wait for 1.0 is my advice.

With my hundreds of hours in Satisfactory, Factorio, Dyson Sphere Program, and a few lesser heard of random factory games, this is my favorite of them all. And to think it's only considered early access... I look forward to what the future brings!

Extraordinarily fun game when you finally get to build a sphere and see it from a close planet.
Tiny install size, runs extremely well, but.... no cloud saves!? I lost my 150+ hour save due to a system reinstall, and not knowing that the saves are local only..
Can run a decent size factory on a very old PC, it does chug at times though.

This is a novel mix between Factorio and Satisfactory, but with enough of its own quirks to give it a unique identity. It takes a bit to understand, but once you have some standard blueprints and get a feel for the flow of resources, you can start reusing the same design patterns and slowly abstract your way up to interstellar-scale gameplay.

This game is much less about perfect ratios and hyper-optimized layouts, and much more about a larger logistics problem that unfolds over time. If you like Factorio or Satisfactory, this is definitely worth a shot.

Polished. Complete. Vast.

It's hard to say what else they could have added. Maybe they could have put more "personality" into the mech, cutscenes, voice overs, etc. It's all gameplay in a technically "perfect" package.

This game is an improved, visually more satisfying Factorió. 10/10 higly recommend!
Things this game needs:
-Multiplayer
-Modding support
-More endgame content
Addicting factory game, best game ever
im sure this is a fun game to play. Unfortunately the dialog print is too small for me to read.. and dont see any options to make it bigger.

Built factory 
left game for a month 
booted it up again
can no longer comprehend my own factory

This game scratches that autistic itch better than any other game. Couple that with some truly breathtaking views - there's nothing quite like seeing a partially-completed Sphere around a blue-white star rise above the horizon - and this is easily one of my favorite games ever.

This game is peak. I have always been a holdout on automation-based games like Factorio, but downloaded this since I love space. This game opened my eyes to the possibilities. It truly is a masterpiece in every sense of the word and a work of love by the developers. The soundtrack is amazing, the ambiance is immersive, the art style is beautiful, the depth of complexity is astounding, and the sheer scale of the star system is mind-boggling considering it is all simulated in real time.

The game has a very forgiving learning curve that even someone with no automation experience like me could get lost in, while providing veterans with immediate areas to min/max or design perfect bases with no bottlenecks. Complexity arises naturally as you progress through the tech tree, but it is in no way linear. I've spent countless hours completely refactoring bases accord just to fix bottlenecks and optimize layouts, and I still feel like I have so much left to do. At one point, my progression was abruptly interrupted when my infrastructure outgrew its power grid, triggering a massive energy crisis. Solving that meant entirely rethinking my setup, deprecating older setups, and performing a massive logistics overhaul to transition onto stable, large-scale solar power grid on a tidally-locked planet. The sheer satisfaction of untangling a massive problem like that and seeing the grid stabilize with is unmatched. There are so many of those moments here.

The enemies are not mindless creatures in this game. Instead, they compete with you by leeching stars of energy, and they aggro when you begin to leech from their energy source. You can confront them head-on or sap them of energy/matter and prevent their growth. I love this kind of freedom because the enemies fit perfectly into the context of the world and you are not forced into large-scale combat unless you want to. They also drop resources, which creates an entirely different type of playthrough centered on combat-farms which is incredibly cool.

I just completed my first Dyson sphere and it was one of the most profound experiences I've had when gaming. From making my own design, to building the infrastructure on that planet and sourcing materials from multiple systems, to the sheer spectacle of it being built, I was completely immersed the entire time. Every individual solar sail must be crafted, transported to an ejector, and fired. That solar sail then travels the entire way into a swarm or into a Dyson sphere shell. My sphere required 2 million sails, and watching my tidally-locked planet firing 50 a second, seeing each of them travel to embed themselves in a swarm or into the Dyson shell, was a surreal, profound experience.

No other game has given me quite the feeling of seeing my hard work materialize and seeing so much smooth simulation occurring at once in such an all-encompassing immersive environment. If you have ever looked up at the stars and wanted to build something truly impossible, buy this game. It is worth every single second. 10/10.

This is definitely one of the best games of all time. If you are a sci fi nerd, this is a MUST play.
So many little details that make this game amazing.
You will look up at the stars, you will hover your mouse over the most luminous ones, and their names you will pop up. Then you tell yourself, "Wow. Thats not just a background star. You can go there." So you spend 40 hours grinding, and those 40 hours pass in no time; by the time you have the warp drive unlocked and can now explore the universe you have forgotten that, in the beginning, thats what you wanted to do. Because this game is just so good at keeping you occupied. Its so good.
So much fun! It's only gotten better and better over the course of early access.
You know how when you go to sleep, wake-up seconds later, and the entire night has passed?  That's what happens when you login to the game.  I don't even know what day it is.
Played for 5 hours straight and my brain got scrambled, good game.
This is probably what it feels like to own an oil tycoon and becoming a bajillionaire while not giving a ♥♥♥♥ about the environment , incredible

As an old Factorio player, I really like the production and logistic interface of Dyson Sphere Program. The controls and game principles are fluid and intuitive. The Dark Fog add an increased difficulty but you can manage these enemies with the in-game parameters. There is a sense of control and progression with the Tech Tree. It gives you goals that you can manage at your own pace. I really recommend this game if you are an automation game fanatic!

As A factorio gamer and discovering this factory game i love it. I like where the direction of this game is going and cant wait for more content. i dont usually start over new games when games like these are still under development, i like to wait till a next big update where there new buildings or ways to redo a factory. The Dark Fog is an awesome way to waste my time or to pass time for factories to start up and get things going or to just go and destroy the whole fleet for some dirt or for fun. Keep it up i will play again once theres another big update cant wait.

This is a strategic game that is similar to the game, Satisfactory. It really gets you to think outside the box and produce things with conveyer belts and development. The 'enemy' feature that has been added from the past couple years is a nice addition (I don't remember it being there when I first played it back in 2021), but picking it up again, now, it really drives greater innovation under the challenge of having to enjoy research with the aspect of having to defend yourself against an aggressive enemy; that is although active, it still is contained. I really like this game because it gets you to pursue innovation not out of necessity and purpose, but out of curiosity, and due to that drive for curiosity, you stumble upon what you really need to advance yourself throughout the game. I believe this preserves the 'fun' factor, and really challenges you to step out of your comfort zone of safety to challenge yourself to grow to new heights of inspiration.

If someone told you that you’d spend hours designing conveyor belt layouts and resources pipelines in the vast reaches of space one day and loving every minute of it then you wouldn’t believe them, but that’s the case for Dyson Sphere Program.

In essence, it is a factory-building, automation game. But to reduce it to that alone seems to trivialize the game. You begin on a single planet where you'll gather resources and begin to erect simple production lines with a gigantic goal waiting to unfold: a Dyson Sphere, a megastructure built around a star. Seeing the sheer scale of this enterprise come to fruition, bit by bit, is the most profoundly rewarding experience I’ve ever played and hits every note of That one Factory Building Game with a beautifully executed science-fiction setting begging for exploration.

Explore you will as you hop from star to star, discovering new worlds with specialized resources and environments and building an interstellar industrial complex that never grows tiresome. The resource-building and planning mechanics are deeply rewarding as everything interconnects with each other and every decision leads you further into the grand design. This, above all else, is what will keep you staring at your screen long after it’s time to turn it off.

I really enjoyed the addition of enemies. Previously the experience could become too relaxed but with a threat always looming over your complex, it makes the strategic thinking feel more necessary. It takes what could be considered a boring simulation game and injects the experience with actual risk. It works.
There are drawbacks, however. It has a learning curve like most games in this genre, and beginners could feel overwhelmed in the first couple hours. It can begin to feel like a bit of a grind in the mid-game when production chains become repetitive until your next big jump of technology. Additionally, though the enemies are enjoyable, it would have been nice if they offered a bit more variety.

If you're a fan of games like That one Factory Building Game and strategy, resource management games, then Dyson Sphere Program is perfect for you. It’s a game for builders and a great addition to your library if a galaxy-spanning factory with a star inside is your idea of fun.

Score: 7/10 - Highly Recommended
I played for over 100 hours, watched over 1000 hours and still am mesmerized by the beauty of the game.
Most relaxing game without getting boring, good for re-plays and just messing around
this game consumes you in every way the same way you consume all the energy of that star and more
works perfect, no bugs, and you have a lot of mods to play, also works in multiplayer, nice game

I only started playing recently, with the Dark Fog update.  I have put hundreds of hours into the game.  I have really enjoyed it.  I would imagine that without the Dark Fog the game would feel kind of empty and less interesting.  If nothing else, it gives you more to do after you've basically built everything you need.  

I'm on my third run.  The first one, I was thoroughly annoyed by dark fog constantly making me run around to put out fires.  It's kind of a pain while also trying to learn the rest of the game.  The second run, I turned down the difficulty and related settings, but I went too far.  I could sneeze on the DF and they would die.  It was nice while I was building, but then I was left with a boring situation.  Now I'm on my third run and I know enough to deal with the DF early without turning down the difficulty.  My point is, be careful about how you decide to set up the DF on a new game.  You can change basic difficulty in-game, but you can't change the rest of the options that you get at game-start.

What I love most about Dyson Sphere Program is how naturally the scale expands.

You start by developing your immediate surroundings, then the rest of your planet, then a nearby moon, then the main planet, then neighboring planets, then another star system — first one moon or planet, then eventually every celestial body in that system — and finally multiple star systems. The sense of progression feels incredibly organic.

The game lets you experience, in a very concrete way, the act of transforming entire environments in order to achieve the grand goal of building a Dyson sphere. There is something fascinating, and almost sinful, about gradually “invading” even nearby star systems for the sake of that objective.

I still remember the feeling of starting on a moon orbiting a gas giant, unlocking flight, circling the world for the first time, and then being pulled by necessity into space. The excitement of flying to a neighboring planet for the first time, the fear of accidentally falling into the gas giant, the vast emptiness I felt while crossing the space between orbital paths, and the tension of warping at incredible speed to another star system — all of those moments left a strong impression on me.

And all of that is accompanied by the sense of relief I feel whenever I return to my home planet.

My production lines have become a complete mess more than once, and I have often thought about abandoning my home planet entirely and starting over in another system. But I still cannot bring myself to cut it loose. Part of it is practical — reusing an existing, complicated production network is often still more efficient than rebuilding everything from scratch. But more than that, I have grown attached to what I have built over dozens of hours.

Above all, the scenery is beautiful.

The game has a bright, almost pop-like visual style, but it also creates surprisingly complex and breathtaking views: the rotation of a moon, its orbit around a planet, and the planet’s own orbit all combining to form ever-changing skies. The surfaces of planets, moons, and stars have a wonderful variety of character. Solar panels drifting in orbit, an editable Dyson sphere under construction, and the spectacular sights of young stars, giants, white dwarfs, black holes, and other special celestial bodies are all amazing to behold.

Dyson Sphere Program is not just a factory-building game. It is a game about expanding your reach, reshaping worlds, and slowly realizing the scale of what you are doing.

I'd say this is the first "solar punk" factory game I've ever played. I think that distinction along with it's quality and really interesting themes and (minor/subtle) story pieces really makes is a standout.
You have a goal, you have opposition, and you have plenty to do. Yet, you have something more fleshed out than if the people making this didn't care.
I appreciate the devs, I appreciate the game, and I appreciate what it has to say about the fact humanity will still make mistakes among the stars- and how that's just another problem we'll solve as well. 

That being said: some genuine multiplayer might be nice. Definitely not a requirement, but I know friends who own this game who like playing it with others. Maybe an update where you can play as an early seeding of entities (I am thinking of a specific group but am unsure if they are spoilers) that individually aren't as capable as the normal character to avoid making the game too easy, but together can be more efficient when played right. Shift the skill requirements from planning and logistics, towards a coordination and communication gimmick. Heck, maybe the more players there are the more specialized you could be.
Just an idea, not one I am strongly attached to either, but I figured the devs already added so much I might as well assume the game is done and spit out ideas that could become a silly mod if nothing else.
I literally destroyed a graphics card on my computer playing this damn thing and I still recommend it.

I started with DSP without even knowing anything about the factory automation genre, and somehow it became one of the most addictive games I’ve played in years. The controls and the UI feel familiar almost immediately, so getting into the game is surprisingly easy even for beginners. Then you start optimizing one production line, then another, and suddenly it was 3 AM.

I’m not recommending this game for anyone with even a minor OCD, because the urge to rebuild and optimize everything never really stops. I restarted it more times than I’d like to admit just because I thought I could make my factory layout “slightly better” the next time.

One of the best factory simulators I've ever played. It's a bit simpler in some areas than Factorio but not to its detriment. The enemies in the game aren't as aggressive so it feels more chill than Factorio. I really enjoy the pacing of the game. Every tier of technology offers something new. Your mech starts off barely able to move and chomping on trees to stay powered up. But by mid to late game you're running on nuclear fuel rods. I highly recommend to anyone looking for a factory simulator that has less urgency than Factorio and a space exploration element.
It is a really fun game. Makes me think of satisfactory. And the fact that they are still updating it is amazing! 8.5/10 :]

The goals are mysterious and not engaging. Just can't connect to the sci-fi aesthetic.

I come from Factorio and Timberborn. Those games feel real and grounded. Dyson Sphere feels like I'm just progressing for the sake of progressing, and checking flashy futuristic check boxes for non existent technologies.

It is a good factory building game. I enjoyed playing through it and even through I prefer satisfactory and factorio space over it, it is a good expereince. I find the enemies to be balanced around certain overpowered strategies which makes them not that interesting to engage with (missile turrents + signal towers). Due to how you can route belts on many different layers builds become very messy, it also make it a lot more difficuilt to automate building compared to satisfactory (building take parts) or factorio where you can have requester chests stock multiple of the same item. Unlike in factorio which makes automating building that take 4 items a pain since the chest are the same size as the constructor. I think there is hidden content related to the enemies but this isn't detailed in game so I didn't explore it, this should probably be addressed. Dyson spheres (strucutre componets) feel like they take a long time to setup and produce in a reasonable capacity I feel like there needs to easier to make earlier, in a similar vein swarms should probably be moved forwards a little. In a similar capacity accumulators discharge / charge rates feel to small compared to the magnitudes of power draw in the later game so a MK2 could be a good thing to add. Early game power generation in the suit for space flight is a annoying thing at least until you get fuel rods as it is easy to run out of fuel and get stuck in space, this alongside with the controls being uninitive. It would be nice if pilesorters and similar had a hold mode to only output there max stack size (like factorio stack inserters). Currently I didn't really feel like doing much in the post-game as there wasn't really anything meaningful to work towards really outside of having to constantly go out and build more resource mining stations which I just felt as kind of a chore unlike in factorio where infinite mining productive makes it partically a non issue it feels the infiinite upgrades in their feasiable levels would still result in a quite often having to rebuild mining sites. I also heard dipole magnets easily run out if you ever utilised them. Overall I had fun, I just didn't binge it like factorio space or satisfactory and came back to it to play a couple hours ever so often.

Dear Devs:
Excellent game... BUT 

The last major release was September 2025. The last major update before that I think was September 2024. The devs have gotten themselves into a hole where they no longer release incremental features and updates.

You need to begin shipping updates regularly. Every month, something, even if it's small that makes a positive impact to the player experience. You're losing your player base and player loyalty because you're shipping slower than a dinosaur developer.

Have seen this game pop up in my feed from various sources and been quite interested in it for a long time. But honestly the QOL and gamedesign leaves A LOT to be desired.

A slower introduction to the game where you get to learn it properly, better default binds for not-american keyboards and allowing us to rebind things to the mouse thumb buttons would go a long way.

This game is wildly unfriendly to interface with. 

The controls are rather unorthodox to a factory game veteran, but not so alien that it makes the game unplayable.
Navigating the ui is generally clunky and requires way too many button clicks and key presses just to find what is needed for a basic task.
The first hour will spam you with hints like "press WASD to move" and two seconds later "by the way you can right click to move!" and then later "And you can hold shift right click to queue up actions independent from your character's origin!"
The game will also spam you with readable tutorials that are far too wordy.

Compare this to a game like Factorio which only teaches the basic concepts needed to play. It leaves the player to discover all the tech like holding shift or ctrl while crafting to make different amounts of an item, or pressing are while belting to rotate your belt line instantly.

Of course comparing a pioneer of the genre to an early access game is a bit absurd. I would just like the devs to put time into reducing friction for new players interfacing with the many complex systems of the game.

This game has loads of potential to be fun and for me to sink a lot of hours into. But for now there are too many quality of life features missing. I'm looking forward to playing it once it's polished and released.

Some annoyances:

- It wasn't completely obvious how to connect buildings to belts when setting up the first smelters. Sorters feel unnecessary
- When going to my first planet, the flight controls really sucked
- Getting around on a new, big planet can be difficult. I kept losing where parts of my factory were. I eventually figured out how to set indicators for patches via the dashboard, but it's really cumbersome and you end up with a persistent icon on the top centre of your screen, taking up prime real estate.
- The UI somehow presents too much information, and too little information
- The keybinds suck

DSP seems to have a decent modding community which can probably resolve some of the QOL issues in the short term, but the mods are currently distributed as DLLs which seems too risky to bother with security-wise. So I hope that when the game eventually releases, there is built in modding support with guardrails.

Unique factory / base builder.
The spherical geometry has some strange behavior but it's a fine twist once you get used to it.
The progression and scale is amazing.
The interplanetary travel doesn't follow accurate orbital mechanics but is some type of modified physics that somehow still feels great.
"Space. It seems to go on and on forever. But then you get to the end and a gorilla starts throwing barrels at you."
Not fun, I usually start playing at 8 and quit at 8:15. Wait why is the sun up

Generic conveyor belt simulator. Seems to be stuck in Mental Early Access with no solution to the boring grind of feeding items into the tech tree. Combat is dull and your Mech lacks any weight or purpose in combat. This isn't a Supreme Commander mech, it's a Toy Story plastic toy. At least the AI drops some token loot when they die. Suffers from the same Oil-is-Everything chemical plant design as most other conveyor belt sims, identical to Factorio etc.

Graphics are great. Gameplay is dull. Planets are lifeless.

It's tough for this game to be in a genre where Factorio and Satisfactory exist, but I'm gonna compare it to them anyways. It sorta feels like a combo of the two, but combined in a bad way. So much is going on in the UI, localization is not good, the addition of the darkfog really doesn't make sense, building feels really weird and the belt and inserter system in this is not good. SOMETIMES you can go out of a building with just a belt, but sometimes you need an inserter too. I'm gonna keep trying it but it's not good enough if you've played either Satisfactory or Factorio.

its 20 bucks. thats really all there is to say. I am a bit disappointed though. The game is clearly attempting to squeeze into the factorio space which its clearly done pretty well, while also distinguishing itself. the issue is that its been out 5 years (save your "early access" BS.. if its available to the public for a fee, its "out") and theres still a lot that needs worked on that IMO are pretty simple fixes to implement and are kind of game breaking. 

These factory style games are heavily fueled by efficiency which means doing things as quickly and efficiently as possible but theres issues with the inserters/sorters where no matter what you do, they place themselve 2, even 3 tiles away from the structure youre attempting to link them to which heavily drops their productivity speed. Internet solutions have been spotty where some say it has to do with the curvature of the planet but i dont buy that cuz ill have several other lines right next to it that all link up perfectly. IDK what it is but things like that are kind big for a game like this. 

Besides that id say the graphical fidelity of the conveyor items could use a facelift, like whats in the game currently is just a placeholder but, again, its been out 5 years. i dont think thats a placeholder at this point. Id say the game has 10/10 potential but is currently sitting somwhere between 4-5 to me.
If you are not in awe when launching your first sails, creating the first Dyson Swarm, we probably should not be friends.

It just doesn't make sense. inputs and outputs for items are extremely unclear and it's hard to figure what kind of factory the game wants me to make. Maybe i'll come back to this game at some point and try it again but satisfactory and factorio are just way better than this.

The game is incredible like 10/10 factory game. However I am not a fan of the amount of development time. Of course what do I know I don't make games. But even still updates take actual ages and the vehicle update seems like its been worked on for years at this point. I don't even see how vehicles will fit into the loop of this game well. I hope one day they shut my ass up and I hope I am surprised but currently it doesn't look good
I'm Colombia, this ♥♥♥♥ is more addictive that cocain. Dont play it for your own sake.

To be honest, I don't know how these early access games end up with Overwhelmingly Positive reviews. This one is so clunky and unintuitive from the get go. It takes a while just to figure out the basics, how to connect buildings, what the upgrades actually do and how to apply them, and what all the different systems do. After coming from a game like Satisfactory or Planet Crafter, this one is something you want to love because it initially looks and feels cool, but can quickly get frustrating. I'm sure with more time, and hours spent reading forums, it'll be fun and interesting, but I'm not there yet and seems like it might be a very long time until I feel that way. No built-in multiplayer is also a huge turn off.
Slow start, but super addictive once you get into it.
amazing game very fun no lag no bug not expensive
One of the best factory games out there, truly worth the money.
Please add more decorations like lanterns that help light your bases up at night
